article.title: Look forward to
article.description: Meaning: to feel excited about something that is going to happen. Example: 'I'm looking forward to the weekend.' Grammar note: 'to' here is a preposition, not part of an infinitive — so it's followed by a noun or gerund (-ing), never a base verb. Common mistake: 'I look forward to see you' is incorrect; correct is 'I look forward to seeing you'.
article.content: Meaning: to feel excited about something that is going to happen. Example: 'I'm looking forward to the weekend.' Grammar note: 'to' here is a preposition, not part of an infinitive — so it's followed by a noun or gerund (-ing), never a base verb. Common mistake: 'I look forward to see you' is incorrect; correct is 'I look forward to seeing you'.

generated.caption:
Ever said "I look forward to see you" and wondered if it sounded quite right? 🤔

When you 'look forward to' something, you're excited for it! The trick? 'To' here is a preposition, so it's followed by a noun or gerund (-ing), never a base verb.

So, say 'I'm looking forward to the weekend' or 'I look forward to seeing you'! ✨

Save this tip, share, and follow @TheEnglishVault for daily lessons!
generated.script: Stop saying 'I look forward to see you!' That's a common mistake. The phrase 'look forward to' means to feel excited about something that is going to happen. But here's the important grammar note: the 'to' in 'look forward to' is a preposition, not part of an infinitive. This means it must always be followed by a noun or a gerund, which is the -ing form of a verb. For instance, you'd say, 'I'm looking forward to the weekend.' So, remember to say 'I look forward to seeing you,' never 'I look forward to see you.' Try using 'look forward to' correctly today! Follow The English Vault for more quick English tips.

slides.heading: What does it mean?
slides.text: To feel excited about something that is going to happen.
slides.hindiMeaning: किसी आने वाली चीज़ के लिए उत्साहित महसूस करना।

slides.heading: The Key Grammar Rule!
slides.text: In 'look forward to', 'to' is a preposition. This means it MUST be followed by a noun or a gerund (-ing verb). It's NOT part of an infinitive here!

slides.heading: See it in action!
slides.text:
I'm looking forward to the weekend. (Here, 'weekend' is a noun.)

I'm looking forward to seeing you. (Here, 'seeing' is a gerund.)

slides.wrong: I look forward to see you.
slides.right: I look forward to seeing you.

slides.text: Ready to level up your English? Follow @TheEnglishVault for daily lessons!
